npcs.name: the Sparrow
npcs.title: a thief of the dead, already inside
npcs.hail: *(a lean shape detaches itself from the shadow of a shelf-stack with the unhurried confidence of someone who got past every lock an hour ago and has been waiting, amused, to see who else the night would wash in — and she grins at you, a thief recognizing a fellow professional, or a fellow problem)*

npcs.name: Brother Codex
npcs.title: the night-archivist, half-asleep
npcs.hail: *(a soft, round Choir clerk dozes at the night-desk with a half-eaten supper and a guttering lamp, the very picture of a man who drew the watch nobody wanted and has made his peace with it by not, in any meaningful sense, watching — though he stirs, now, at the cold draught of you)*

npcs.name: the Great Tally
npcs.title: the master ledger of the Faithless
npcs.hail: *(it sits alone on a lectern of black iron at the chamber's heart, a book the size of a door, bound in something you choose not to examine closely, and the air around it is *cold* in your particular way — because every name in it is a soul promised to the Wall, and the book *knows* you are the one kind of reader who can feel them in there, pressed against the inside of the cover like breath on a window)*

variants.text: *(She looks you over, and her grin sharpens with professional interest.)* …Well, *well.* *(she keeps her voice to the burglar's murmur that doesn't carry)* You've the *crossroads* dust on you. You've been to see the *old clerk* at the *wayshrine.* *(a soft laugh)* Then we're after the *same* prize, you and I, by *different* roads — because there's *one* book in all this *causeway* the dust at the crossroads can't reach, and it's *that* one. *(she nods at the great black lectern)* The *Great Tally.* The Choir's *master* list of who's *forfeit.* *Jergal* can strike a *name* — *one* at a time, for a *piece* of you. But the *Tally* — *(her eyes gleam)* — the *Tally* is *every* name at *once*, and it's sitting in an *unlocked* room because the Choir never *dreamed* a soul would be *fool* enough to *want* it. *(she offers a thin hand)* I'm the *Sparrow.* I steal what the *dead* can't keep. And I think, *cold one*, you and I should *talk* before either of us *touches* that book.

*(She doesn't reach for a blade — she reaches for a conversation, which from a professional is more unsettling.)* …Easy, easy. *(the burglar's murmur, all charm)* If you meant to *raise* the house you'd have done it at the *door*, and you didn't, which tells me you're *here* for the *same* reason I am, which means we are — for the *moment* — *colleagues.* *(she gestures at the great black book on its lectern)* The *Sparrow.* Best *second-storey* soul on the causeway, living *or* dead. I came for the *Great Tally* — the Choir's *master* ledger, every *Faithless* name they mean to *wall*, all in *one* spine. *(her eyes flick over you, calculating)* And *you* came for it too, didn't you, *cold one.* I can *smell* the *want* on you. *(a thin grin)* The *question* — the *only* question that *matters* in a room like this — is whether we're going to *help* each other *carry* it out… or *race* each other *to* it. *Choose* careful. I'm *charming* either *way*, but only *one* way ends with us *both* still *holding* all our *secrets.*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She leans against a shelf-stack, perfectly at ease in a place that should terrify anyone, and watches you with bright professional curiosity.)* So. *(a murmur)* Talk fast and talk *quiet* — the *clerk's* dozing, not *deaf.* What's the *Tally* to *you*, cold one? And what are we to *each other*, the next *five* minutes?

choices.text: "Who hired you, Sparrow? A book like that has a buyer. Tell me who wants every Faithless name in one place, and I'll tell you whether we're colleagues."
"Partners, then — for tonight. You're past the locks, I can handle the clerk and the cold. We get the book out together and settle who keeps it once it's clear of the house."

[INSIGHT] "You're not really asking to split it, are you. You're reading whether I'm soft enough to turn my back on. Professional to professional: I'm not. The second that book's in hand you'll make your play for it, because a buyer who'd pay for *that* doesn't pay for *halves.* So let's drop the dance — I know you'll cross me, you know I know, and now we both work *honestly* inside *that.*"

*(let the one who can feel the souls in the book speak plainly)* "Understand what you've come to steal, Sparrow. That's not a ledger — it's ten thousand souls in a cage of paper, and I'm the one kind of thief who can *feel* them in there. Whoever your buyer is, they don't want a *book.* They want a *leash* on every Faithless soul in the Realms. I'm not letting that book leave this room to wear *anyone's* collar — yours, the Choir's, or your buyer's."
"We're nothing to each other, Sparrow. Stay out of my way and we'll both walk out of this." *(turn toward the book)*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She weighs whether to answer, and professional pride wins — she likes a colleague who asks the right question.)* …Smart. *Always* ask who pays. *(a murmur)* I don't *have* a name — buyers like *this* don't *give* one — but I have a *shape.* The coin came through three hands, each *colder* than the last, and the *last* hand smelled of *incense* and *old* paper. *(she watches you)* Which means my buyer is *Choir.* Or *was* Choir. Someone *inside* the machine who wants the *master* list *out* of the *Counting-House* and into a *private* hand — and you don't *want* that, cold one, unless you mean to run the *Wall* as your *own* concern. *(her grin fades a notch)* I'll be *honest*, since you asked *straight*: I took this job for the *coin* and didn't *think* about the *cargo.* I *steal* jewels, mostly. *Letters.* The odd *relic.* *(she glances at the great black book)* I've never *stolen* ten thousand *people* before. And now that I'm *standing* here, in the *cold* of it, I find I *like* it *rather* less than I *priced* it.

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She clasps your hand, quick and warm and entirely untrustworthy, and grins.)* …*Partners.* *(a murmur)* For *tonight.* I do *love* a clean word on a dirty job. *(she's already moving, all business)* Here's the *lay*, then, colleague: the *clerk's* the only *watch* — there's *no* guard on the *book* itself, because the Choir's whole *defense* is that *nobody* would *dare.* The *locks* are *mine*, already *picked.* What's *left* is the *clerk* — *Brother Codex*, soft as a *bun*, dozing at the *desk* — and the *cold* off the *book*, which is *your* department, not mine; I can't so much as *touch* the spine without my *teeth* aching. *(she eyes you)* So *you* handle the *clerk* and the *cold*, I'll handle the *getaway*, and we'll *settle* the *keeping* of it once we're *clear.* *(a wink that does not reach her calculating eyes)* …*Settle* it *fair.* Obviously. *(she absolutely means to cross you, and she absolutely knows you know, and the partnership is, in the way of all such partnerships, perfectly understood.)*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(For a heartbeat the charm drops entirely, and what's underneath is sharp and surprised and — grudgingly — respectful.)* …*Hah.* *(a real laugh, low)* Well. *That's* the dance over quick. *(she spreads her hands, caught and not minding)* You're *right*, of course. Buyer like mine pays for the *whole* book or *nothing*, and the *second* it's in a hand I'd make my play, because that's the *job*, and the *job's* the *job.* *(she tilts her head)* But you've done a *clever* thing, cold one, naming it *out loud* — because now I *can't* cross you by *surprise*, and a cross you *see* coming isn't a *cross*, it's a *negotiation.* *(her grin returns, changed — warmer, more dangerous)* So let's *negotiate.* You *clearly* don't mean to *sell* the thing — you've the look of a soul with a *cause*, gods help you. Tell me what you mean to *do* with ten thousand names, and maybe — *maybe* — I find I'd rather be *part* of *that* story than collect a *fee* for handing the *Wall* to a *worse* owner. *Surprise* me, cold one. I'm *expensive*, but I'm not, it *turns* out, *cheap.*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She laughs lightly and the charm never wavers — you reached for the read and closed on smoke.)* …*Suspicious.* *(a murmur)* And here I thought we were getting *on.* *(she spreads her hands, all innocence)* I'm *wounded*, cold one. I offer an *honest* night's *partnership* and you *squint* at me like I've already got the *book* under my *coat.* *(a wink)* Which I *don't.* Yet. *(she pushes off the shelf)* Tell you what — let's *not* spoil a *promising* evening with *trust* issues. You do *your* part, I'll do *mine*, and we'll see who's *standing* where when the *book* comes *off* the *lectern.* *(lightly, brightly, giving you nothing)* *Colleagues.* That's all I *said.* I never said *friends.*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She goes still. Whatever she expected from the cold soul, it wasn't *this* — and her gaze drifts to the great black book with something new in it, something close to unease.)* …You can *feel* them. *(quietly, the charm set aside)* In the *book.* *(she looks at the Tally, and for the first time the professional ease cracks)* I *priced* this job as *paper*, cold one. A *heavy* book, a *cold* room, a *fat* fee. And you're telling me I've been *standing* here *casually* beside *ten thousand* — *(she doesn't finish)* — *(a long breath)* …I've stolen a *lot* of things. I've a *rule*, if you'd believe a *thief* has *rules*: I don't steal *people.* *Slavers'* coin I've *turned* down *flat*, fee be *damned.* And I walked in *here* and *didn't see* that *that's* — *(she presses her lips thin)* — that's *exactly* what the *Tally* is. A *slaver's* manifest. *Ten thousand* souls in a *cage*, and a *buyer* who wants the *cage's* key. *(she meets your eyes, and the calculation in hers has changed targets)* …I'm *out*, cold one. Of the *buyer's* job. *(a thin, fierce grin)* But I'm *in* — if you'll *have* a *reformed* second-storey *soul* — on *yours.* Whatever you mean to *do* with that *book* that *isn't* selling *ten thousand* people, I'd *like* to help you *do* it. *Call* it *conscience.* I'm as *surprised* as *you* are to *find* I packed *some.*

nodes.speaker: the Sparrow
nodes.text: *(She raises both hands, easy, and melts a half-step back into the shelf-shadow, unoffended.)* …*Lone* wolf. *(a murmur, almost approving)* Fair *enough*, cold one. I *work* alone *too*, mostly — it's *cleaner.* *(but her eyes track you toward the book, bright and unhurried)* I'll just — *watch*, then. From *here.* See how you *handle* the *clerk*, and the *cold*, and the *choice* a book like *that* puts to a soul. *(a thin grin)* Because there *is* a *choice*, cold one, at the *lectern* — *steal* it, *burn* it, *something* cleverer — and *whichever* you make, I'll *learn* something *worth* more than my *fee* just *watching* a *Returned* make it. *(she settles into the dark)* Go *on.* Don't mind *me.* I'm only the *second*-best *thief* in this *room* now, and I'm *professional* enough to *admit* it. *Mostly.*

variants.text: *(He blinks awake, knocks a crumb off his robe, and squints at you with the bleary alarm of a man who drew the one watch where nothing ever happens and now something is.)* …Wh— who's — *(he fumbles for a little brass bell, then thinks better of ringing it before he knows whether he should)* …You're not *staff.* *(he peers)* But you walk like you *belong*, which is — *(he swallows)* — there's *talk*, you know. Up top. That the *Canon* herself has gone *strange* — locked her *office*, dismissed the *scriveners*, hasn't *signed* a *reckoning* in *days.* *(his voice drops, frightened and gossipy at once)* And *now* a *cold* stranger walks into the *deep* archive at the *dead* of night like they've *every* right. *(he wrings his soft hands)* …Are you — are you *her* doing? Some *audit?* Some *purge?* Should I be *ringing* this *bell*, friend, or should I be very *carefully not* ringing it? I *genuinely* cannot *tell*, and getting it *wrong* either way is how a man ends up *in* the *books* instead of *minding* them.

*(He jerks upright, supper sliding off his knee, and gropes for a small brass bell with the panic of a soft man who has never once had to actually do the job he is paid for.)* …Halt! *(it comes out as a squeak)* Halt, and — and *identify* — *(he gets a proper look at the cold of you and his nerve wavers)* — oh. Oh, you're one of the *odd* ones. The *cold* ones. *(he does not ring the bell; he clutches it)* We're — we're *not supposed* to have *callers*, in the *deep* archive, at *night.* That's rather the *point* of *deep*, and of *night.* *(he wets his lips)* I'm *Brother Codex.* I *mind* the *books.* I don't — look, I'll be *plain*, because I'm *bad* at the *other* thing: I am *one* soft clerk with a *bell* and a *cold soul* between me and the *most* dangerous *book* in the *Choir*, and I am *trying* to decide, very *quickly*, whether you are a *problem* I should *ring* about — *(he glances at the bell)* — or a *problem* I should very *quietly* let *be someone else's.* *Help* me decide, friend. *Gently.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(He keeps the little bell half-raised, a soft man's whole defense, and watches you the way a rabbit watches a thing it hasn't decided is a fox.)* …So. *(a nervous swallow)* What's it to *be*, cold one? I'd *dearly* love this to end with me *finishing* my *supper* and *you* being *somewhere else.* Talk me *toward* that.

choices.text: [DECEPTION] *(stronger if the Canon's gone strange)* "Lower the bell, Brother. I'm the audit. The Canon sent me down to pull the master Tally before it can be tampered with — surely you've heard she's locked her office and stopped signing. Why do you think? She knows the book's compromised. I'm here to secure it. Now stand aside, or your name goes in my report next to 'obstructed the Canon's own auditor.'"

[STEALTH] *(don't argue — slip past while he fumbles the bell)* "..." *(you let his nervous chatter cover the soft scrape of your boots as you circle the shelf-stacks toward the lectern, keeping the cold of you low and the shadows between you)*

*(he's another unbought clerk, like Pell — turn him with the truth only the Returned can swear to)* "Put the bell down, Brother. I'll tell you the truth, because I think you're the sort who'd rather hear it: that book is a list of ten thousand souls promised to the Wall for the crime of kneeling to no god. You *mind* it. You've never *read* it — not really, not what it *is.* I have. I walked out of that Wall wearing its cold, and I can *feel* every name in your book waiting in there. I'm here to take it out of the Choir's hands. You can ring that bell and stay a man who guards a cage. Or you can look the other way and be the man who let it open."

[INTIMIDATION] "Ring that bell, Brother Codex, and I promise you'll be the first name the cold finds in this room. Look at me. Look at what I am. I walked out of the Wall you keep the list for. You're a soft man with a supper and a bell, and I am the thing your whole Choir built that list to keep *out.* Sit down. Eat. See nothing."
"Easy, Brother. I'm going. No trouble." *(step back toward the stair)*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(The bell sinks. Relief floods his soft face — not at the lie, but at being handed a version of events in which he does not have to be brave.)* …An *audit.* *(he nods, fast, eager to believe)* Yes — *yes*, that — that makes *sense* of it, doesn't it, the Canon going *strange*, the *book* needing *securing* — *of course* she'd send someone *cold*, someone the *Wall* can't *touch*, to *handle* a *compromised* — *(he's already shuffling aside, gathering his supper)* — I *said* to myself, I said, *'Codex, something's afoot up top,'* and here you *are*, *proof* of it. *(he gestures at the lectern, almost grateful)* *Take* it. *Secure* it. *Please.* I'll *witness* in the *night-book* that the *Canon's auditor* came and *signed* it *out*, all *proper.* *(he beams, weak with relief)* And — between us, friend — *thank* you. I've not *slept* right since they *posted* me down here with that — that *thing.* It *hums*, at night. Did you know? *Cold* little *hum*, like a *room* full of *held* breath. I'll be *glad* to see the *back* of it. You're doing me a *kindness*, whatever the *paperwork* says.

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just claim the audit — you *perform* it: you produce a scrap of parchment, frown at it like a checklist, ask him to confirm the Tally's shelf-mark and the date of its last verification, and tut at his answer. By the time you finish, Brother Codex is not standing aside — he is *assisting.*)* …Shelf-mark *Aleph-null*, last verified — *(he checks a docket, flustered)* — *Goodmonth*, friend, which is — *gods*, that's *months*, that's *well* past the *re-verification* cycle, no *wonder* the Canon's — *(he's appalled at his own institution)* — here, *here*, let me *help* you lift it, the *spine's* a *terror*, mind your — oh, you don't *feel* the cold, do you, that's *why* they *sent* you, that's *brilliant* — *(he's actually carrying one corner toward you)* — and I'll *sign* the *night-book* AND the *transfer* docket AND I'll *note* the *verification* lapse so it's *clear* the *fault* was *up top* and not with *me*, because — *(he lowers his voice, conspiratorial)* — frankly, friend, I've *wanted* this *cursed* book *out* of my *watch* since the *day* I drew it, and you are the *first* official soul to *agree* with me. *Take* it with my *blessing* and the *Choir's* own *seal* on the *paperwork.* I'll make it *airtight.* You were *never* even *here* irregularly — you were here *correctly.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(He doesn't ring the bell — but he doesn't lower it either, and a stubborn clerkish caution surfaces under the fear.)* …An *audit.* *(he frowns)* With no *docket?* No *seal?* No *writ* with the *Canon's* mark? *(he clutches the bell tighter)* I'm — forgive me, friend, I'm *soft*, but I'm not *daft*, and an *auditor* comes with *paper*, that's rather the *whole* of *auditing*, it's *paper* doing *paper* to *paper.* *(he wavers)* You *might* be *telling* it true — the Canon *has* gone *strange*, that part's *real* — but you might *also* be a *cold thief* who *heard* the *gossip* and is *using* it, and I *can't* tell, and the *book* is — *(he glances at the bell, at the lectern, at you)* — *talk* me *past* the *paper*, cold one, or I'm going to do the *one* brave thing a *coward* can, which is *ring* this and let *braver* souls *sort* it.

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(💀 You declare yourself the Canon's auditor with great authority — and then, pressed for the Canon's name, produce the wrong one, confidently calling High Measurer Mereth 'Canon *Merrin*,' and, compounding it, refer to the Counting-House as 'the Countinghaus' in an accent that belongs to no part of the Realms. Brother Codex's soft face does something complicated.)* …*Merrin.* *(flatly, for a frightened man)* The Canon's name is *Mereth.* *Mereth Vael.* I've *filed* under it for *nine years.* *(he is, slowly, standing up)* And it's the *Counting-House*, not the — whatever you just — *(his hand tightens on the bell)* …You know, friend, an *auditor* would know the *name* of the *woman* who *sent* him. An auditor would know the *building.* *(he swallows, and finds, somewhere in the terror, a thin reed of spine)* I think you are a *cold liar* who is *very bad* at *lying*, and I think I am about to ring this *bell*, and I think you should run, because I may be *soft* but I am *about* to be *soft* and *loud.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(He natters on at the empty air where you were standing — '...so really if you'd just state your business...' — while you are already three shelf-stacks away, the cold of you sliding through the dark between the ledgers, and by the time he realizes the room has gone quiet you are at the lectern and he is still squinting at the spot you left.)* …Hello? *(uncertainly, behind you, far off)* …Friend? Did you — did you *go?* *(a pause, then, to himself, with enormous relief)* …Oh, *thank* the *Powers*, they *went.* *(the scrape of him settling back to his supper)* Just the *cold* playing *tricks.* The *book* does that — makes the *dark* feel *peopled.* *(he never sees you at all; the score is yours to make in silence.)*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(✦ You don't just slip past — you read his little world as you go: the bell on its string, the half-eaten supper, the guttering lamp. A breath on the lamp-flame at the right moment, a soft sound from the *wrong* direction, and the soft clerk gets up, fretting, to investigate a draught two stacks away — leaving you a clear, unhurried path to the lectern and all the time in the world.)* …Is someone — *(his voice recedes, moving away from you, chasing the sound you planted)* — is someone *there?* By the *Crown-Wars* shelf? *(shuffling, peering, entirely the wrong way)* …I *do* wish the *cold* wouldn't — *hello?* *(he is far across the room now, muttering to the wrong shadows, his back to you and the book and the whole quiet triumph of it)* …*Drafts.* It's *always* drafts. I shall *write a memorandum* about the *drafts.* *(you have, effectively, all night.)*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(Your boot finds the one loose board in the whole archive — of course it does — and the soft clerk's head snaps round, and for one frozen instant you and he stare at each other across the ledgers.)* …*There!* *(the bell comes up)* You're — you're *creeping*, that's — *honest* souls don't *creep*, that's the *one* thing I *know* about *creeping!* *(his hand shakes around the bell, but it's *raised*)* Now you *stop* — you *stop* right *there*, cold one — I *will* ring this, I *will*, I don't *want* to, I *hate* a *fuss*, but I will *not* be the clerk who *let* a *creeper* at the *Tally* on his *watch* — *(he is terrified and he is, against all odds, not backing down)* — *talk*, *quick*, and talk *honest*, because the *creeping's* spent and the *bell's* a *finger-twitch* from *waking* the *whole* house.

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(The bell lowers, slowly, as you speak — and something in the soft round face shifts from fear to a kind of stricken recognition, the look of a man hearing aloud a thing he has felt in the dark and never let himself think.)* …*Ten thousand.* *(quietly)* I *knew* it *hummed.* I *told* myself it was the *cold*, the *draught*, the — but you're *saying* it's *them.* The *names.* *(he looks at the great black book with new horror)* I've *minded* it *nine* years, cold one. *Dusted* it. *Turned* its *pages* to keep the *damp* out. And I *never* once let myself *count* what a *page* of it *was.* *(his voice cracks)* Because if I *counted* — if I *let* myself *know* I was the *nightwatch* on a *cage* of *ten thousand* godless *souls* — I could not have *drawn* the *wages*, and the *wages* feed my *mother*, and so I *minded* the *book* and *un-knew* the *book*, every *night*, on *purpose.* *(he sets the bell down on the desk, deliberately, like laying down a weight)* …I'll not *ring* it. *(barely)* I'll do *worse* than *not* ring it. I'll *sleep.* Right *here*, *deep* and *sudden*, the way a *soft* tired clerk *does* — and I'll *wake* having *seen* *nothing*, and *whatever's* *gone* from that *lectern* by *morning*, I'll *swear* on my *mother* I *slept* through. *(he meets your eyes, and there's a terrible relief in his)* *Take* it. *Open* it. *Free* them. And let me be — just *once*, just *tonight* — the man who *guarded* the *door* by *failing* to. It's the *bravest* thing a *coward* gets *offered*, and I'll not *waste* it.

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(He sits. Hard. The bell slips from his fingers and rolls under the desk, and he makes no move to chase it — a soft man's nervous system overruling whatever loyalty he was paid for.)* …Sitting. *(a squeak)* I'm — *sitting.* See? *Seated.* *Supping.* *(he picks up his cold supper with trembling hands and takes a pointed, terrified bite)* I see — I see *nothing.* There's — there's *nobody* in the *deep* archive tonight, that'd be — that'd be *absurd*, who'd *come* down *here* — *(he will not look at the lectern, will not look at you)* — I'm just a *soft* man *finishing* his *bread*, and the *cold* I *feel* is the *draught*, the *draught*, it's *always* the *draught* — *(barely, to his supper)* — *please* don't — I've a *mother*, cold one, I'm *no* hero, I *never* signed for *heroics*, I signed for a *wage* and a *quiet* watch — *take* whatever you — just — be *gone* before the *morning* relief, and let me *keep* the *one* thing I've *got*, which is *not* being *brave* enough to *die* for a *book* I *hate.*

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(You loom, and you are cold, and you are everything the list was written to stop — and the soft man's terror, instead of folding, *spikes*, the blind animal panic of a cornered thing, and his hand convulses around the bell.)* …*No* — no-no-no — *(the bell jangles, once, sharp and loud in the dead-quiet archive, the sound rocketing up the stair toward the sleeping house)* — I'm *sorry*, I'm *sorry*, you *frightened* me, I *ring* when I'm *frightened*, it's a *reflex*, I've *always* — *(he claps both hands over his mouth, appalled at himself, as a distant answering shout drifts down from somewhere above)* — oh, *gods*, I've *done* it now, I've *rung* it — you'd best *hurry*, cold one, whatever you *came* for, because *soft* as I am that *bell* is *loud*, and there are *less* soft souls *upstairs* who'll have *heard* it. *(wretchedly)* I didn't *mean* — you just — you can't *menace* a *coward* and expect *quiet*, it's the *one* thing we're *bad* at —

nodes.speaker: Brother Codex
nodes.text: *(The relief that washes over him is total, and faintly comic — a man handed back the quiet evening he'd resigned himself to losing.)* …*Going.* *(he sags)* Oh, *bless* you, cold one, *yes*, *going* is — *going* is *perfect*, *going* is my *favourite* thing a *caller* has ever *done.* *(he's already settling back, retrieving his supper)* No *trouble*, no *bell*, no *fuss*, no *book* gone *missing* on *Codex's* watch. *(he beams weakly)* I'll *not* even *mention* you. Why would I? *Nothing* *happened.* A *cold* draught came down the *stair* and *went* back up it, and I *finished* my *bread.* *(he lifts the supper in a small, grateful salute)* Mind the *steps* on your *way*, friend. They're *worn.* The *Choir* won't *fix* them. The *Choir* won't fix *anything* down *here* — it's all *up-top* marble and *down-here* *rot.* …Rather like the *rest* of it, now I *say* it *aloud.*

variants.text: *(The clerk is handled; the room is yours; and you stand at last before the great black book on its iron lectern. It does not speak — books do not speak — but your dead-touched sense fills the silence it leaves, because the cold pouring off it is not the cold of *paper.* It is the cold of *ten thousand held breaths.* You open the cover, and the first page is a sea of names in the Choir's cramped hand, and as your eyes fall on them you *feel* each one — a washerwoman, a ferryman, a midwife, a child — every soul the Choir has promised to the Wall, pressed against the inside of the binding like faces against a frozen window, and every one of them, the instant your cold touches the page, goes very still, and *hopes.* Whatever you do at this lectern, you do it to *all* of them at once.)*

*(You stand before the great black book, but the clerk is not yet handled and the room is not yet yours — and the book waits, vast and patient and full, while somewhere behind you the night-watch could still raise the house. You can feel the souls in it, ten thousand held breaths pressed against the binding, but a score taken in haste is a score half-made. Best to settle the watch before you settle the book — unless you mean to gamble the whole tally on speed.)*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(The book lies open. Ten thousand names. Your cold hand rests on the page, and every soul in the binding holds its breath, and the choice — the real one, the one the whole night was for — is yours to make.)*

choices.text: *(burn it — free them all, now, at any cost)* "No more list, no more Wall to feed it. I'm burning it. Every name out of the cage at once — let the Choir try to mortar souls it can no longer *find.*"

*(take it to the one clerk who can empty it safely)* "I'm not burning ten thousand anchors and praying. I'm taking this to the dust at the crossroads — Jergal, who *invented* the ledger and knows how to un-write a name without unmooring the soul. He can balance every line in this book honestly, one at a time, faster than the Choir can re-copy it. The Tally doesn't get destroyed. It gets *audited empty.*"

*(don't take the book — take the lie under it)* "I don't need the whole book. I need the *first page.* The founding instrument — Venn's forged seal, the dead clerk's hand that started all of this. Cut *that* out, and every name after it rests on nothing. The Tally becomes ten thousand entries in a ledger with no lawful authority. I leave the book and steal the *fraud.*"

*(let the one who can feel them choose for them)* "Ten thousand of you, and not one consulted on what's done with your names. So I'll ask, the only way I can — with the cold that hears you. You don't want a thief for a new owner, or a fire that scatters you, or a hero's grand gesture that gets you killed twice. You want *out*, quietly, all of you, in a way that *holds.* I felt the answer the moment I touched the page. I'll carry you to the dust, and let him write you free, name by name, until the book is blank."

*(grab it and run — the watch is up, there's no time for the clever play)* "No time. The bell's rung, the house is waking — I take the whole book and I run, and I sort out the saving once it's clear of the Choir's hands. A messy rescue beats a tidy damnation."
*(step back from the lectern)* "..." *(you lift your cold hand from the page; the ten thousand breaths hold a moment longer, then settle — not yet, not tonight)*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(You take the guttering candle from the desk and touch it to the open page, and for one heartbeat nothing — and then the great black book catches, not with ordinary fire but with a cold blue light, and as the names curl and blacken you feel them come *loose*, all at once, ten thousand held breaths exhaling into the dark.)* *(It is not clean. Your dead-touched sense reels with it — some of the freed go *up*, glad and gone, but others, the ones too far un-witnessed already, simply *scatter*, unmoored, with no name and no anchor to hold them in the world, sliding toward the same grey nothing the Wall fed on. You have broken the cage. You have not, you understand with a cold lurch, saved everyone in it — a list is also a *tether*, and you have cut ten thousand tethers at once, and the strong souls fly free and the frail ones simply… drift.)* *(But the book is ash. The Choir has no master list. They cannot mortar a soul whose name they no longer hold — and from this night the Wall takes no new Faithless that this book would have named. A brutal mercy, paid by the frailest, bought for all the rest. The Sparrow, somewhere behind you, lets out a long, low breath. "...Gods," she murmurs. "You don't do things by halves, cold one.")*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(You close the great book, and lift it — it weighs like a door, like ten thousand small refusals to be forgotten — and you carry it up out of the deep archive and out into the night, toward a dusty crossroads where an old clerk keeps a desk.)* *(Your dead-touched sense, all the long way, is full of them: the names in the binding, no longer holding their breath but *waiting* now, the way a queue waits, patient and hopeful. You are not their owner. You are their *courier* — carrying ten thousand souls from the hand that caged them to the one hand in all the Realms that can let them go *gently*, one true line at a time, balanced against the debts the cosmos already owes them.)* *(Behind you the Sparrow watches you heft the impossible book and shakes her head, half in awe, half in professional despair. "Hardest score on the causeway," she murmurs, "and you're not *fencing* it, you're *delivering* it to a *dead bureaucrat* to be *paperwork'd* out of existence. ...That's the most *honest* theft I've ever seen, cold one. I didn't know the trade *had* one.")*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(You do not lift the heavy book at all. You turn instead to its *first* page — the founding instrument, the Concord that gives the whole tally its authority — and there at its foot, in the cramped hand of a four-centuries-dead clerk, is the seal: V-E-N-N, a living man's forgery of a god's command, the single thread the entire edifice hangs from.)* *(You cut it free — one page, neatly excised — and the change is immediate and silent and total. Your dead-touched sense feels it ripple back through the binding: the ten thousand names are *still there*, still inked, still listed — but the *authority* under them has gone *out*, like a lamp, and what was a *lawful register of the forfeit* is now simply *a list of names in a book a thief could write.* Without the founding seal, no reckoning that cites it can stand; no soul it names can be *lawfully* walled. You have not freed them with fire or carried them to a clerk. You have *un-made the law* and left the *evidence* of the crime sitting on its own lectern.)* *(The Sparrow leans in to look at the single stolen page in your cold hand, and a slow, delighted, professional grin spreads over her face. "...You came into the most guarded room in the Choir," she breathes, "and stole *one page.* And it's the page that makes the other ten thousand *worthless.* Cold one — I have been *robbing* people for *decades*, and I have never once seen a score that *elegant.* Teach me that. Whatever *that* was. Teach me.")*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(You rest both cold hands on the open page, and you do the thing no one has ever done for the souls in this book: you *ask* them. Not aloud — there are no ears in a binding — but with the cold that hears, the dead-touched sense the Wall sharpened in you, reaching into the ten thousand held breaths and *listening* for what they want.)* *(And they answer. Not in words — in a single vast, quiet *exhale* of preference, ten thousand souls leaning the same way at once: not the fire, which frees the strong and scatters the frail; not a new owner, however kind; but *out*, gently, in an order that *holds*, each name un-written true so that *no one drifts.* They want the *patient* mercy. They want the *clerk.* They want, you realize with a cold ache, exactly what the frailest among them *needs* — which the strong ones, who'd survive the fire, have chosen *for* them, the way a good queue lets the weakest go first.)* *(You close the book gently, and lift it, and carry ten thousand consulted souls toward the crossroads and the dust — and your sense is full of something it has never carried before: not their fear, not their hope, but their *consent.* The Sparrow falls into step beside you, quiet for once. "...You asked them," she says, wondering. "Nobody asks the cargo, cold one. In my whole trade — nobody *ever* asks the cargo." A pause. "...I think I've been in the wrong line of work.")*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(The bell still rings in your ears and the house is waking above, and there is no time for elegance — so you do the brute, honest thing: you heave the great black book off its lectern, tuck ten thousand souls under one cold arm, and *run.*)* *(The Sparrow is already at the stair, and to her credit she does not bolt without you — "This way, the coal-chute, MOVE, cold one!" — and you go, the impossible book banging against your hip, Choir voices shouting somewhere behind, out through a soot-black gap and into the night with the master ledger of the Faithless stolen *whole.*)* *(It is messy. It is loud. You have no plan yet for ten thousand names and a hundred angry clerks who now *know* the book is gone. But your dead-touched sense, even at a dead run, is full of the souls under your arm — and they are not frightened. They are *thrilled.* For the first time since they were inked into that cage, the book that holds them is *moving*, in the *wrong* direction, *away* from the Wall, carried by a *cold* soul who *ran* into the Choir's own heart to *take* them. A rescue half-planned beats a damnation fully filed. You'll sort the rest in the dark. You always do.)*

nodes.speaker: the Great Tally
nodes.text: *(You lift your cold hand from the page, and the great book lies open and unharmed, ten thousand names still caged in its binding, ten thousand held breaths settling slowly back into the long patient dark they've waited in for years.)* *(Not tonight. The score is real, the room is open, the book is *here* — but a thing this large, done wrong, costs more than leaving it done at all, and some part of you wants the *right* tool, the *right* hour, the *right* plan before you decide the fate of ten thousand souls in a single cold breath.)* *(The Sparrow watches you step back from the most valuable object on the causeway, and instead of scorn there's something thoughtful in her face. "...Walking away from the score," she murmurs. "Hardest move in the trade. Most thieves can't do it — the want gets them." She glances at the book. "It'll keep. The Choir doesn't know we were here, if your clerk holds his tongue. Come back when you know what ten thousand names are *for*, cold one. I'll likely still be lurking. I find I'm... invested.")*
